title: Will Iraq’s New Prime Minister Really Take On Corruption?
gist: Iraq's new, weak PM Ali al-Zaidi is riding a popular anti-corruption drive that has seized $106M+ against an estimated $2 trillion looted since 2003, but the campaign looks like score-settling; his most likely path is a balancing act, aided by US pressure on Iran-aligned militia financing.

insights.claim: Iraqi raids since late June seized more than $106 million in embezzled cash under 'Operation Dawn,' with a legal advisor estimating Iraq has lost roughly $2 trillion to corruption since 2003—equivalent to at least 15 years of total government expenditure.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: Quantifies the staggering scale of Iraqi state capture—context for any Iraq investment or reconstruction thesis.

insights.claim: Iraq scored 28 out of 100 on Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index last year—its best ranking in more than a decade.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: A low but improving governance baseline against which the new campaign will be judged.

insights.claim: The arrests largely target officials affiliated with predecessor Sudani and the Azm bloc, making 'Operation Dawn' look feeble at best or aimed at settling scores at worst.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: Selective enforcement suggests factional score-settling, not systemic reform—tempers optimism.

insights.claim: PM Ali al-Zaidi is a weak compromise candidate who had never held office and is personally overseeing the anti-corruption cases to swiftly build domestic and international support.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: A politically fragile PM staking legitimacy on anti-corruption—success or failure shapes Iraq's stability.

insights.claim: The most likely scenario is Zaidi striking a balance—expanding investigations but not targeting all the complicit—to keep his fragile government intact, rather than either full escalation or dropped charges.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: Base case is partial, self-limiting reform—don't expect transformative anti-corruption outcomes.

insights.claim: The US launched 'Operation Economic Fury' targeting Iran-aligned militia financing, pressuring the Iraqi government and banks to go after those groups' funds.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: US financial pressure on Iraqi banks and militias is a compliance and sanctions-risk factor for Iraq exposure.

insights.claim: Zaidi is due to visit Washington this month seeking to show Trump he is capable, while the US wants to bring business to Iraq—making corruption curbs crucial to that goal.
insights.speaker: Mina Al-Oraibi
insights.so_what: Ties Iraqi reform momentum to US business interest—watch the Washington visit for deal signals.
